title: Turner Syndrome
oneLiner: A common chromosomal disorder (45,X or mosaic) causing short stature, gonadal dysgenesis, and congenital heart/aortic anomalies in phenotypic females, requiring lifelong surveillance.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Aortic dissection/rupture** — especially in those with bicuspid aortic valve/coarctation. *Finding:* sudden severe tearing chest/back pain + unequal arm BPs or radial-femoral delay.
- **Hypertensive crisis from coarctation of aorta** — *Finding:* BP >180/110 in arms with weak femoral pulses and delayed capillary refill in legs. Risk of stroke or left ventricular failure.
sections.Presentation:
- **Infants:** Congenital lymphoedema (dorsum of hands/feet), webbed neck, low hairline, broad chest with widely spaced nipples.
- **Children:** Short stature (growth velocity <5th centile), recurrent otitis media, ptosis, nail dysplasia, cubitus valgus.
- **Adolescents/Adults:** Delayed puberty, primary amenorrhea, infertility, neck webbing, cardiac murmur (bicuspid aortic valve), hypertension.
- **Key exam:** Check BP in both arms and one leg (coarctation), palpate femoral pulses (delay), auscultate for ejection systolic murmur (bicuspid valve), inspect for webbed neck, low hairline, shield chest, wide-spaced nipples.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Noonan syndrome** — autosomal dominant, normal karyotype, pulmonary stenosis (not bicuspid aortic), pectus carinatum/excavatum, characteristic facies (hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures).
- **Short stature with pubertal delay** (constitutional delay, coeliac, hypothyroidism) — no webbed neck or cardiac anomalies; karyotype normal.
- **Primary ovarian insufficiency** (e.g., 46,XX gonadal dysgenesis) — normal stature and cardiac exam; karyotype normal.
- **Splitter:** Karyotype (45,X or mosaic) confirms Turner; no other test reliably differentiates.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (LVH, conduction defects), BP four-limb measurement, pulse oximetry.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, TFT (autoimmune hypothyroidism common), anti-TPO, anti-TG.
  - Coeliac screen (tTG-IgA + total IgA).
  - FSH, LH (elevated in ovarian failure).
  - Karyotype (if not already done; lymphocyte or skin fibroblast).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Echocardiogram (aortic root diameter, bicuspid valve, coarctation).
  - **Second-line:** Renal ultrasound (horseshoe kidney). Cardiac MRI if aortic dilation or coarctation on echo.
- **Special tests:**
  - Audiometry (conductive/sensorineural hearing loss common).
  - DEXA scan from age 18 (osteoporosis risk).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Karyotype showing 45,X or 46,X with structural abnormality (isochromosome, ring X) / mosaic. FSH >40 IU/L in a teenager with amenorrhea strongly suggests ovarian failure.
- **Never-miss test:** ***Echocardiogram*** — missing aortic root dilation or coarctation can lead to sudden death.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If chest/back pain with BP asymmetry → **urgent CT aortogram** ± IV beta-blocker (e.g., labetalol) per local protocol for aortic dissection.
  - If severe hypertension + radiofemoral delay → IV antihypertensives (e.g., labetalol, GTN infusion) as per [check local policy]; arrange urgent cardiology review for coarctation stenting/surgery.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start **growth hormone (somatropin)** if newly diagnosed child (refer to paediatric endocrinology for dose and monitoring — typical dose 45-50 mcg/kg/day SC).
  - **Oestrogen replacement** for pubertal induction (usually from age 12-13; e.g., ethinylestradiol 2-5 mcg daily or conjugated oestrogens titrated up over 2-3 years; add progestogen after 2 years of unopposed oestrogen or when breakthrough bleeding occurs).
  - Monitor BP daily; start antihypertensives if BP >95th centile for age (e.g., amlodipine, lisinopril). Check thyroid function, glucose, coeliac serology if not already done.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - GH therapy until final height (usually age 14-16, or when growth velocity <2 cm/yr).
  - Lifelong oestrogen/progestogen HRT (oestrogen-only if no uterus, combined if uterus present).
  - Annual BP, TFT, hearing test; echo every 1-2 years (sooner if aortic root dilation).
  - DEXA scan every 2-3 years if osteoporotic.
  - Fertility counselling (oocyte donation, surrogacy).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** None specific unless for associated conditions. For a short-stay, use standard IV fluids (e.g., 5% dextrose/0.45% saline if nil by mouth). Observation: 4-hourly NEWS2.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Somatropin (growth hormone):** Dose per local paediatric endocrinology protocol (typically 45-50 mcg/kg/day SC). Do not start without specialist guidance.
- **Oestrogen (pubertal induction):** Ethinylestradiol 2-5 mcg po daily, titrate up over 2-3 years. Alternative: 17β-estradiol patches (starting 6.25 mcg/24h twice weekly).
- **Progestogen (after 2 years oestrogen or breakthrough bleeding):** Norethisterone 5 mg po daily for 12 days per month, or medroxyprogesterone 5-10 mg daily for 12 days per month.
- **Bisphosphonates (if osteoporosis and not on HRT):** Alendronic acid 70 mg po once weekly (check renal function; avoid in eGFR <35). Dose per BNF.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - GH: active malignancy, papilloedema, diabetic retinopathy.
  - Oestrogen: pregnancy, active VTE, oestrogen-sensitive cancer.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** GH reduced dose in hepatic impairment; oestrogens reduced/avoid in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **GH overdosing** → risk of pseudotumor cerebri (severe headache, vomiting, papilloedema) and slipped capital femoral epiphysis (hip pain, limp). **Oestrogen overdose** → thromboembolism (DVT/PE). Always start low and titrate.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Aortic dissection/rupture.
  - Hypertensive encephalopathy (from coarctation).
  - Pseudotumor cerebri (GH therapy).
  - Thromboembolism (oestrogen therapy).
- **Chronic:**
  - Short adult stature (without GH).
  - Infertility.
  - Osteoporosis (untreated ovarian failure).
  - Autoimmune disease (hypothyroidism, coeliac, IBD, vitiligo).
  - Sensorineural hearing loss, conductive deafness (otitis media).
  - Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney → UTIs, hypertension).
  - Metabolic syndrome (obesity, insulin resistance, T2DM).
sections.Prognosis: - Normal life expectancy if cardiovascular risks are managed. Most women have normal cognition (though higher risk of social/executive difficulties). Infertility is near-universal (except rare mosaic with ovarian function). With GH therapy, adult height can reach low-normal range.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform four-limb BP in any girl with short stature** — you may pick up undiagnosed coarctation.
- **Check karyotype on any newborn with unexplained lymphoedema or webbed neck** — early detection prevents delays in growth hormone and cardiology follow-up.
- **Screen all Turner patients for coeliac disease (tTG-IgA) and hypothyroidism (TSH/anti-TPO)** at diagnosis and every 2-3 years.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't start GH without first ruling out cardiac anomaly** — aortic root dilation is a relative contraindication until cardiology clearance. If aortic root diameter >2.0 cm/m² BSA, refer to cardiology before GH.
- **Don't omit progestogen when prescribing oestrogen to a patient with a uterus** — unopposed oestrogen increases endometrial cancer risk.
- **Don't assume all short girls with delayed puberty have constitutional delay** — send a karyotype before starting oestrogen or GH.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe headache, vomiting, or visual changes (pseudotumor cerebri on GH).
  - Sudden chest/back pain, palpitations, or syncope (aortic dissection).
  - Uncontrolled hypertension (BP >150/95 on home monitoring).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Paediatric endocrinology (for age <18) / adult endocrinology (≥18) — for GH + puberty.
  - Cardiology — annual review (echo, BP).
  - Audiology — annual hearing test.
  - Gynaecology — fertility counselling; if on HRT, review every 1-2 years.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/registrar [Reg] if:**
  - BP >160/100 despite first-line oral antihypertensives.
  - Any new chest pain, back pain, or syncope — rule out dissection (urgent CT).
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology.
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, or lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Suspected aortic dissection with haemodynamic instability.
  - Refractory hypertension (BP >180/110 not responding to IV therapy).

rawMarkdown:
## Turner Syndrome
**One-liner:** A common chromosomal disorder (45,X or mosaic) causing short stature, gonadal dysgenesis, and congenital heart/aortic anomalies in phenotypic females, requiring lifelong surveillance.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Aortic dissection/rupture** — especially in those with bicuspid aortic valve/coarctation. *Finding:* sudden severe tearing chest/back pain + unequal arm BPs or radial-femoral delay.
- **Hypertensive crisis from coarctation of aorta** — *Finding:* BP >180/110 in arms with weak femoral pulses and delayed capillary refill in legs. Risk of stroke or left ventricular failure.

### Presentation
- **Infants:** Congenital lymphoedema (dorsum of hands/feet), webbed neck, low hairline, broad chest with widely spaced nipples.
- **Children:** Short stature (growth velocity <5th centile), recurrent otitis media, ptosis, nail dysplasia, cubitus valgus.
- **Adolescents/Adults:** Delayed puberty, primary amenorrhea, infertility, neck webbing, cardiac murmur (bicuspid aortic valve), hypertension.
- **Key exam:** Check BP in both arms and one leg (coarctation), palpate femoral pulses (delay), auscultate for ejection systolic murmur (bicuspid valve), inspect for webbed neck, low hairline, shield chest, wide-spaced nipples.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Noonan syndrome** — autosomal dominant, normal karyotype, pulmonary stenosis (not bicuspid aortic), pectus carinatum/excavatum, characteristic facies (hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures).
- **Short stature with pubertal delay** (constitutional delay, coeliac, hypothyroidism) — no webbed neck or cardiac anomalies; karyotype normal.
- **Primary ovarian insufficiency** (e.g., 46,XX gonadal dysgenesis) — normal stature and cardiac exam; karyotype normal.
- **Splitter:** Karyotype (45,X or mosaic) confirms Turner; no other test reliably differentiates.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (LVH, conduction defects), BP four-limb measurement, pulse oximetry.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, TFT (autoimmune hypothyroidism common), anti-TPO, anti-TG.
  - Coeliac screen (tTG-IgA + total IgA).
  - FSH, LH (elevated in ovarian failure).
  - Karyotype (if not already done; lymphocyte or skin fibroblast).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Echocardiogram (aortic root diameter, bicuspid valve, coarctation).
  - **Second-line:** Renal ultrasound (horseshoe kidney). Cardiac MRI if aortic dilation or coarctation on echo.
- **Special tests:**
  - Audiometry (conductive/sensorineural hearing loss common).
  - DEXA scan from age 18 (osteoporosis risk).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Karyotype showing 45,X or 46,X with structural abnormality (isochromosome, ring X) / mosaic. FSH >40 IU/L in a teenager with amenorrhea strongly suggests ovarian failure.
- **Never-miss test:** ***Echocardiogram*** — missing aortic root dilation or coarctation can lead to sudden death.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If chest/back pain with BP asymmetry → **urgent CT aortogram** ± IV beta-blocker (e.g., labetalol) per local protocol for aortic dissection.
  - If severe hypertension + radiofemoral delay → IV antihypertensives (e.g., labetalol, GTN infusion) as per [check local policy]; arrange urgent cardiology review for coarctation stenting/surgery.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start **growth hormone (somatropin)** if newly diagnosed child (refer to paediatric endocrinology for dose and monitoring — typical dose 45-50 mcg/kg/day SC).
  - **Oestrogen replacement** for pubertal induction (usually from age 12-13; e.g., ethinylestradiol 2-5 mcg daily or conjugated oestrogens titrated up over 2-3 years; add progestogen after 2 years of unopposed oestrogen or when breakthrough bleeding occurs).
  - Monitor BP daily; start antihypertensives if BP >95th centile for age (e.g., amlodipine, lisinopril). Check thyroid function, glucose, coeliac serology if not already done.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - GH therapy until final height (usually age 14-16, or when growth velocity <2 cm/yr).
  - Lifelong oestrogen/progestogen HRT (oestrogen-only if no uterus, combined if uterus present).
  - Annual BP, TFT, hearing test; echo every 1-2 years (sooner if aortic root dilation).
  - DEXA scan every 2-3 years if osteoporotic.
  - Fertility counselling (oocyte donation, surrogacy).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** None specific unless for associated conditions. For a short-stay, use standard IV fluids (e.g., 5% dextrose/0.45% saline if nil by mouth). Observation: 4-hourly NEWS2.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Somatropin (growth hormone):** Dose per local paediatric endocrinology protocol (typically 45-50 mcg/kg/day SC). Do not start without specialist guidance.
- **Oestrogen (pubertal induction):** Ethinylestradiol 2-5 mcg po daily, titrate up over 2-3 years. Alternative: 17β-estradiol patches (starting 6.25 mcg/24h twice weekly).
- **Progestogen (after 2 years oestrogen or breakthrough bleeding):** Norethisterone 5 mg po daily for 12 days per month, or medroxyprogesterone 5-10 mg daily for 12 days per month.
- **Bisphosphonates (if osteoporosis and not on HRT):** Alendronic acid 70 mg po once weekly (check renal function; avoid in eGFR <35). Dose per BNF.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - GH: active malignancy, papilloedema, diabetic retinopathy.
  - Oestrogen: pregnancy, active VTE, oestrogen-sensitive cancer.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** GH reduced dose in hepatic impairment; oestrogens reduced/avoid in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **GH overdosing** → risk of pseudotumor cerebri (severe headache, vomiting, papilloedema) and slipped capital femoral epiphysis (hip pain, limp). **Oestrogen overdose** → thromboembolism (DVT/PE). Always start low and titrate.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Aortic dissection/rupture.
  - Hypertensive encephalopathy (from coarctation).
  - Pseudotumor cerebri (GH therapy).
  - Thromboembolism (oestrogen therapy).
- **Chronic:**
  - Short adult stature (without GH).
  - Infertility.
  - Osteoporosis (untreated ovarian failure).
  - Autoimmune disease (hypothyroidism, coeliac, IBD, vitiligo).
  - Sensorineural hearing loss, conductive deafness (otitis media).
  - Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney → UTIs, hypertension).
  - Metabolic syndrome (obesity, insulin resistance, T2DM).

### Prognosis
- Normal life expectancy if cardiovascular risks are managed. Most women have normal cognition (though higher risk of social/executive difficulties). Infertility is near-universal (except rare mosaic with ovarian function). With GH therapy, adult height can reach low-normal range.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform four-limb BP in any girl with short stature** — you may pick up undiagnosed coarctation.
- **Check karyotype on any newborn with unexplained lymphoedema or webbed neck** — early detection prevents delays in growth hormone and cardiology follow-up.
- **Screen all Turner patients for coeliac disease (tTG-IgA) and hypothyroidism (TSH/anti-TPO)** at diagnosis and every 2-3 years.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't start GH without first ruling out cardiac anomaly** — aortic root dilation is a relative contraindication until cardiology clearance. If aortic root diameter >2.0 cm/m² BSA, refer to cardiology before GH.
- **Don't omit progestogen when prescribing oestrogen to a patient with a uterus** — unopposed oestrogen increases endometrial cancer risk.
- **Don't assume all short girls with delayed puberty have constitutional delay** — send a karyotype before starting oestrogen or GH.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe headache, vomiting, or visual changes (pseudotumor cerebri on GH).
  - Sudden chest/back pain, palpitations, or syncope (aortic dissection).
  - Uncontrolled hypertension (BP >150/95 on home monitoring).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Paediatric endocrinology (for age <18) / adult endocrinology (≥18) — for GH + puberty.
  - Cardiology — annual review (echo, BP).
  - Audiology — annual hearing test.
  - Gynaecology — fertility counselling; if on HRT, review every 1-2 years.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/registrar [Reg] if:**
  - BP >160/100 despite first-line oral antihypertensives.
  - Any new chest pain, back pain, or syncope — rule out dissection (urgent CT).
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology.
- **Call ICU/anaesthetics if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, or lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Suspected aortic dissection with haemodynamic instability.
  - Refractory hypertension (BP >180/110 not responding to IV therapy).
